subject: [PATCH v4 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)

reviews.author: Bernd Schubert
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Bernd Schubert expressed confusion about the buffer selection logic, specifically asking for an explanation of how the patch determines which queue has available buffers in a 3D matrix of CPUs, buffer sizes, and buffers.

reviewer pointed out that if a fuse server starts with a single buffer per queue and scales up, aggregation would be lost
reviews.raw_body:
I'm pretty much lost. It is a 3 dimensional matrix, made of cpus,
buffer-size and buffers. I really want to see a logic how you figured
which queue has free buffers.

---

Depends, if fuse server starts with 1 buffer per queue and slowly scales
up, you basically get exactly one buffer per queue. That is the end of
aggregation.


Thanks,
Bernd

reviews.author: Joanne Koong (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the dispatch logic for non-IO and IO queues can be simplified by reusing existing logic from a previous patch series, and agreed that separate bufpools per queue would be more complicated.

Author pushed back on reviewer's concern that having multiple queues of the same buffer size would be an issue, stating that it would just be normal queue selection and not a problem.
reviews.raw_body:
We already have logic for this (done in your reduced queue series). I
think we could just reuse that logic. A basic implementation would be
having a separate bitmap for non-IO queues vs IO queues, and then once
you figure out if the request is an IO or non-IO request, the rest of
the dispatch logic would be the same for choosing a queue from either
the non-IO queue pool or the IO queue pool. If you're concerned about
dispatch complexity, I think separate bufpools per queue ends up being
more complicated.

---

I really don't think servers will start with 1 buffer per queue.

Happy to discuss this together in the call later today.

Thanks,
Joanne

2026-06-18.patch_summary: This patch adds support for io-uring buffer pools to the fuse file system, allowing userspace to pass a chunk of memory to the kernel and offloading management logic. The approach taken is to implement buffer rings, which can be used for both metadata operations and backend IO requests. However, the need for multiple pools per queue is questioned, with the suggestion that dynamically adding new queues could provide more concurrency and capacity as needed.
